passageOrStimulus: A city council argues that installing more streetlights in a district will reduce nighttime theft because thefts are more common on unlit streets.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: Most thefts in the district occur in daylight near busy markets.
Several streets in the district are already well lit.
Most nighttime thefts in the district are committed by repeat offenders who target homes with unlocked doors.
Residents prefer brighter streets for walking after dark.
The new streetlights would use LED bulbs.

explanation: The argument assumes that lack of lighting is the primary cause. If nighttime thefts are mostly committed by repeat offenders targeting unlocked doors, more lighting will have little effect on this type of crime.

passageOrStimulus: A bakery claims that switching from paper to reusable containers increased customer loyalty because repeat purchases rose after the switch.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: The bakery also began opening earlier on weekdays.
Survey data show that customers who used the reusable containers were more likely to return than customers who did not, even after controlling for spending level.
Nearby competitors lowered their prices during the same period.
Some customers complained that the reusable containers were heavier than paper boxes.
The bakery changed its logo at the same time.

explanation: The fact that customers using reusable containers return more frequently, even after controlling for other variables, directly links the policy to loyalty and strengthens the causal inference.

passageOrStimulus: A fitness studio argues that extending its evening classes will increase annual profit because the classes are usually full.
stem: The argument assumes that

choices.text: the studio can hire instructors for the additional classes without raising total costs so much that profit falls
the studio's evening classes are more profitable per session than its morning classes
members who attend the new evening classes will renew their memberships
no competing studio will respond by extending its own evening classes
the evening classes have been full for several consecutive months

explanation: For additional full classes to actually increase profit, one must assume that the incremental cost of those classes does not offset the gain in revenue.

passageOrStimulus: A survey found that in a small town, households that subscribed to the public library borrowed more books per month than households that did not subscribe.
stem: Which of the following can be properly inferred?

choices.text: Subscribing to the library caused households to read more books.
No households without a subscription borrowed books.
On average, subscribers borrowed more books per month than non-subscribers in the sample.
The town has more libraries than bookstores.
All subscribers read exactly the same number of books.

explanation: The statement provides an average comparison within the sample. A causal conclusion cannot be drawn, but the average difference itself is directly established.

passageOrStimulus: A restaurant's lunch revenue increased even though the number of lunch customers fell.
stem: Which of the following best explains the discrepancy?

choices.text: The restaurant's dinner revenue also increased during the period.
The average amount spent per lunch customer increased substantially.
The restaurant lowered the prices of its most popular lunch dishes.
The lunch customers who did come visited the restaurant more frequently than before.
Fewer customers meant servers could give each table more attention.

explanation: If fewer customers generate more revenue, the average spending per customer must have increased significantly.

passageOrStimulus: A company claims that a four-day workweek improved productivity because total output per employee rose after the policy began.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the claim?

choices.text: Whether employees preferred working four days rather than five
Whether the company's sales department also changed managers
Whether output per employee rose more than it did at similar companies that kept a five-day workweek
Whether the company used more laptops after the policy began
Whether the company offered free coffee on Fridays

explanation: Comparing the trend to that of similar companies makes it possible to judge whether the observed increase exceeds what would have been expected without the new policy.

passageOrStimulus: A student concludes that because every marathon winner trained at least five days per week, training five days per week is sufficient to win a marathon.
stem: The student's reasoning is flawed because it

choices.text: confuses a necessary condition with a sufficient condition
draws a conclusion about all runners from data on winners alone
overlooks other factors, such as talent, that also contribute to winning
assumes that training fewer than five days per week cannot produce a win
treats training frequency as the only measure of a runner's preparation

explanation: Observing that a condition is always present among winners does not support the claim that it is sufficient to guarantee victory.

passageOrStimulus: A publisher argues that releasing a paperback edition of a novel six months after the hardcover will increase total profits.
stem: Which assumption is required?

choices.text: hardcover readers never buy paperbacks
the paperback release will not significantly reduce hardcover sales before the hardcover has already generated most of its revenue
the novel won awards after publication
the publisher owns all printing presses in the country
the novel's author prefers paperbacks

explanation: The argument requires that the paperback release not heavily cannibalize hardcover sales before those sales have generated most of the revenue.

passageOrStimulus: Researchers found that students who slept at least eight hours before an exam scored higher than students who slept less. Therefore, schools should not schedule major exams after very early-morning classes.
stem: The conclusion is that

choices.text: students who sleep at least eight hours score higher on exams than those who sleep less
very early-morning classes reduce the amount of sleep students get
schools should avoid scheduling major exams after very early-morning classes
schools should require students to sleep at least eight hours before an exam
getting more sleep is the most effective way for students to raise their scores

explanation: The normative conclusion concerns the exam schedule; the rest serves as support.

passageOrStimulus: A town expects tax revenue from new apartment buildings to exceed the cost of added services.
stem: This expectation is most likely justified if

choices.text: the new apartments will raise property values throughout the town
the apartments will be occupied by households that pay more in taxes than they require in additional services
the new apartments will be fully occupied within a year of completion
the cost of providing town services has risen more slowly than tax revenue in recent years
the town's tax rate on apartments is higher than the rate charged in nearby towns

explanation: For the fiscal balance to be positive, the housed households must generate more in taxes than they cost in additional services.

passageOrStimulus: A museum increased admission prices, yet attendance rose the following month.
stem: Which of the following, if true, best resolves the paradox?

choices.text: The museum launched a highly popular exhibit that month.
The price increase applied only to adult tickets, not to children's tickets.
Several visitors said the higher prices made them less likely to return.
Attendance at a competing museum across town fell during the same month.
The added ticket revenue allowed the museum to repaint several of its galleries.

explanation: A highly popular exhibition can increase demand enough to offset the price increase.

passageOrStimulus: An analyst says that a city's bike-share program reduced traffic congestion because traffic speeds rose near stations where bike use was highest.
stem: Which of the following best strengthens the argument?

choices.text: The city also widened several roads near the stations.
Speeds rose most on days and at times when bike-share usage was highest, but not in comparable areas without stations.
Many residents own bicycles already.
The stations were painted the same color.
Some riders used the bikes for recreation.

explanation: The temporal and spatial correlation with the stations, combined with the absence of a comparable effect elsewhere, reinforces the idea that the program contributed to the increase in speeds.

passageOrStimulus: A manufacturer claims that a new quality-check system reduced defects because the defect rate fell after the system was introduced.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: The system required workers to attend training sessions.
The factory produced fewer units after the system was introduced.
A separate change in raw-material suppliers improved product quality at the same time.
The defects that remained were easy to repair.
Supervisors preferred the new system.

explanation: A parallel improvement tied to raw materials provides an alternative explanation for the decline in defects.

passageOrStimulus: All members of a chess club attend weekly practice. Some members of the club are also members of the debate team.
stem: Which statement must be true?

choices.text: All debate-team members attend weekly practice.
Some club members attend weekly practice.
No club members are on the debate team.
Every person who attends weekly practice is in the club.
The debate team meets weekly.

explanation: If there are club members, and all club members attend the weekly practice, then some people necessarily attend that practice.

passageOrStimulus: A grocery store manager claims that placing apples near the entrance will increase apple sales because the apples will be seen by more customers.
stem: The claim assumes that

choices.text: customers who see the apples are at least somewhat willing to buy apples
more customers will pass the entrance than currently pass the produce aisle
the apples displayed at the entrance will stay fresh throughout the day
apples are the store's most profitable item per unit sold
moving the apples will not reduce sales of the items previously near the entrance

explanation: Being seen increases sales only if the customers who are exposed can become potential buyers.

passageOrStimulus: A city health department argues that a campaign encouraging handwashing reduced flu cases in schools.
stem: Which evidence would most strengthen the argument?

choices.text: The schools with the biggest rise in handwashing also had the biggest drop in flu cases, while schools without the campaign did not show a similar drop.
Teachers said they liked the posters.
The campaign cost less than expected.
Students missed fewer classes for other reasons.
Flu cases vary from year to year.

explanation: A targeted correlation between increased handwashing and a drop in cases, absent elsewhere, provides the strongest causal support.

passageOrStimulus: A hotel claims that installing more elevators reduced wait times because guests now spend less time on each floor.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: The hotel also added more signs in the lobby.
The elevators were installed during the off-season, when fewer guests arrived.
The wait times fell equally in a neighboring building that did not add elevators.
The elevators are quieter than the old ones.
Guests prefer corner rooms.

explanation: If wait times also decrease in a control building without additional elevators, the decrease cannot be attributed to the new elevators.

passageOrStimulus: A company infers that because employees who worked remotely reported higher satisfaction, remote work causes higher satisfaction.
stem: The reasoning is flawed because it

choices.text: confuses correlation with causation
overlooks the possibility that more satisfied employees were the ones allowed to work remotely
relies on employees' self-reports of how satisfied they were
fails to define what counts as higher satisfaction
ignores whether remote work also affected employee productivity

explanation: Higher satisfaction among remote workers does not establish that remote work is the cause.

passageOrStimulus: A bookstore's membership program increased the number of members, but total membership fees collected decreased.
stem: Which of the following best explains the discrepancy?

choices.text: The program attracted many members who paid a lower introductory fee.
Many of the new members rarely bought books after joining.
A number of longtime members let their paid memberships lapse during the period.
The store spent more on advertising the membership program than it had before.
The new members on average lived farther from the store than existing members did.

explanation: A drop in the average price or in promotional fees could increase membership while reducing total revenue.

passageOrStimulus: A city says a new bus route is successful because ridership has grown every month since launch.
stem: Which question is most useful to ask in evaluating that claim?

choices.text: Have riders said they are satisfied with the new bus route?
Would ridership have grown by a similar amount without the new route?
Does the new route earn enough in fares to cover its operating costs?
Has ridership on the city's other bus routes also grown since the launch?
Did the city add the new route in response to resident requests?

explanation: It is necessary to determine whether the observed growth exceeds what would have occurred anyway.

passageOrStimulus: A software firm claims that publishing a free basic version will increase revenue from its paid version because more users will try the product.
stem: Which assumption is required?

choices.text: many free users will later decide that the paid version is worth buying
the free version will attract more users than any paid product the firm has released
users of the free version will recommend it to friends who do not yet use the product
the cost of supporting free users will be lower than the cost of supporting paying users
the paid version offers features that most users will find useful

explanation: The freemium model works only if a sufficient share of free users ultimately converts to paying customers.

passageOrStimulus: The lab notes show that every sample stored at room temperature spoiled within 24 hours, and some samples stored in a refrigerator remained usable after 24 hours.
stem: Which statement is supported?

choices.text: All refrigerator-stored samples remained usable after 24 hours.
Some room-temperature samples remained usable after 24 hours.
At least one sample stored in a refrigerator remained usable after 24 hours.
No sample spoiled in the refrigerator.
Room temperature is safe for all samples.

explanation: The statement explicitly says that some refrigerated samples remained usable after 24 hours.

passageOrStimulus: A chain of gyms argues that a new app feature increased renewals because members who used the feature renewed at a higher rate.
stem: Which evidence best strengthens the argument?

choices.text: Members who used the feature attended the gym more often than those who did not.
Members who used the feature renewed more often than similar non-users, even after controlling for attendance and membership length.
Overall renewal rates at the chain rose in the year the feature was launched.
The members who used the feature had longer memberships than those who did not.
Most members said they were satisfied with the app after the feature was added.

explanation: Controlling for other factors makes the association more credible as a true effect of the feature itself.

passageOrStimulus: A small airline argues that offering free checked bags increased customer satisfaction because complaint rates fell after the policy began.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: The airline also shortened its ticket-buying process.
The complaint rate fell on routes where the airline had recently improved seat spacing, whether or not free bags were offered.
Passengers like to travel with luggage.
Some customers read the new policy online.
The airline changed the color of the baggage tags.

explanation: A parallel improvement on certain roads offers an alternative explanation for the decline in complaints.

passageOrStimulus: Because the city's new recycling bins were placed near apartment buildings rather than in parks, more glass was collected from apartment areas than from parks. Therefore, the city should place future bins where residents actually generate waste.
stem: The main conclusion is that

choices.text: the new bins collected more glass from apartment areas than from parks
placing bins near apartment buildings increases total recycling
future bins should be placed where residents actually generate waste
the city should add more recycling bins overall next year
bins placed in parks collect less waste because fewer residents visit them

explanation: The final recommendation is the main conclusion.

passageOrStimulus: A theater says it can raise revenue by reserving premium seats for advance online purchases.
stem: The argument depends on the assumption that

choices.text: customers who buy early will pay enough extra to offset any loss from seats that would otherwise be sold later at lower prices
all customers use smartphones
the theater's lobby is small
actors prefer premium seats
online payments are never refunded

explanation: Reserving premium seats increases revenue only if the perceived price premium compensates for any potential loss of standard-price sales.

passageOrStimulus: A town's unemployment rate fell, but the number of people working also fell.
stem: Which of the following best explains the apparent paradox?

choices.text: The labor force shrank because many people stopped looking for work.
Many laid-off workers found new jobs in neighboring towns.
The town's total population grew during the period.
Several employers in the town reduced their workers' hours.
More residents took second jobs to supplement their income.

explanation: The unemployment rate can fall if people leave the labor force, even when the number of jobs decreases.

passageOrStimulus: An energy company claims that replacing old bulbs with LEDs in its offices lowered electricity bills.
stem: Which evidence would most strengthen the claim?

choices.text: The offices had the same number of employees as before.
After the replacement, electricity use fell in the offices while it stayed about the same in similar offices that did not replace bulbs.
Employees said the new bulbs looked brighter.
The company bought bulbs in bulk.
The weather was colder that month.

explanation: Comparing with similar offices that did not switch helps isolate the effect of the LED lights.

passageOrStimulus: A university argues that shorter lectures improve learning because students in the pilot course scored higher than students in the usual course.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: The pilot course was taught by the university's most experienced professor, while the usual course was not.
Students in the pilot course had more breaks between classes.
The pilot course covered a different subject.
Students liked the shorter lectures.
The classroom was renovated.

explanation: A difference in teacher provides a plausible alternative cause for the increase in scores.

passageOrStimulus: A supermarket says that moving checkout counters to the front of the store reduced shopping time because customers now exit faster.
stem: Which question is most useful to evaluate the claim?

choices.text: Did customers report being more satisfied with their shopping experience after the change?
Did total shopping time also fall at comparable supermarkets that did not move their checkout counters?
Did the time customers spend waiting in the checkout line decrease after the change?
Did the number of customers visiting the store rise after the checkout counters were moved?
Did moving the checkout counters require the store to reduce its shelf space?

explanation: Comparing with similar supermarkets makes it possible to determine whether the decline is due to the change or to other factors.

passageOrStimulus: No winter jackets are sold in July, and some coats are sold in July.
stem: Which statement must be true?

choices.text: Some coats are winter jackets.
At least one coat sold in July is not a winter jacket.
All coats are sold in July.
No coats are sold in winter.
Winter jackets are never sold.

explanation: Since no winter jacket is sold in July, any coat sold in July cannot be a winter jacket; since some coats are sold in July, at least one coat is not a winter jacket.

passageOrStimulus: A warehouse wants to reduce damage during shipping.
stem: Its best strategy is to switch to stronger packaging if

choices.text: the stronger packaging costs less than the average value of damaged goods it would prevent
customers dislike plain boxes
shipping times are already short
the warehouse stores only fragile items
the stronger packaging is recyclable

explanation: The strategy is profitable if the cost of the new packaging is less than the average value of damages avoided.

passageOrStimulus: A charity received fewer individual donations this year, but total donation revenue increased.
stem: Which of the following best explains the discrepancy?

choices.text: Fewer donors gave much larger average donations.
The number of donors fell by a larger percentage than revenue rose.
The charity received more donations from first-time donors this year.
Donations from previous years had been steadily increasing.
The charity's expenses rose along with its revenue.

explanation: Fewer individual donations can produce more revenue if the average amount per donation has increased substantially.

passageOrStimulus: A theater manager argues that starting shows 15 minutes earlier will increase attendance because commuters will find it easier to arrive on time.
stem: Which assumption is required?

choices.text: theatergoers who commute are an important share of potential attendees
all performers dislike earlier start times
the theater sells snacks
the new start time will reduce ticket prices
parking is free near the theater

explanation: The argument depends on commuters representing a large enough share of the audience to influence total attendance.
